Types of Social Group
The simplest social structure Diamond proposes is the band, comprising just a few dozen members:
Bands lack many institutions that we take for granted in our own society. They have no permanent single base of residence. The band’s land is used jointly by the whole group, instead of being partitioned among subgroups or individuals. There is no regular economic specialization, except by age and sex: all able-bodied individuals forage for food. There are no formal institutions, such as laws, police, and treaties, to resolve conflicts within and between bands. {Diamond, 1998 #14}[pp. 268-7]
Diamond continues:
Band organisation is often described as “Egalitarian”: there is no formalized social stratification into upper and lower classes, no formalized or hereditary leadership, and no formalized monopolies of information and decision making. However, the term “egalitarian” should not be taken to mean that all band members are equal in prestige and contribute equally to decisions. Rather, the term merely means that any band “leadership” is informal and acquired through qualifies such as personality, strength, intelligence, and fighting skills. {Diamond, 1998 #14}[pp. 269]
One level of social complexity above is the tribe, with typical numbers in the hundreds. These differ from the bands by “usually having fixed settlements.” {Diamond, 1998 #14}[p. 270] 
[A] tribe also differs [from a band] in that it consists of more than one formally recognized kinship group, termed clans, which exchange marriage partners. Land belongs to a particular clan, not to the whole tribe. However, the number of people in a tribe is still low enough that everyone knows everyone else by name and relationships. {Diamond, 1998 #14}[p. 271]
Within both bands and tribes: 
Their economy is based on reciprocal exchanges between individuals of families, rather than on a redistribution of tribute paid to some central authority. Economic specialization is slight: full-time crafts specialists are lacking, and every able-bodied adult … participates in growing, gathering, or hunting food. {Diamond, 1998 #14}[p. 272]
Chiefdoms, with typical group sizes in the thousands, are the next level of societal complexity:
The most distinctive economic feature of chiefdoms was their shift from reliance solely on the reciprocal exchanges characteristic of bands and tribes, by which A gives B a gift while expecting that B at some unspecified future time will give a gift of comparable value to A. … While continuing reciprocal exchanges and without marketing or money, chiefdoms developed an additional new system termed a redistributive economy. A simple example would involve a chief receiving wheat and gradually fiving it out again in the months between harvests. {Diamond, 1998 #14}[p.275]
A Note concerning Mauss, Bourdieu and Diamond
The categories Diamond suggests are heuristic, and in a sense arbitrary: there is no objective point at which a tribe becomes a chiefdom. It is perhaps for this reason that even this ‘distinctive economic feature’ of the redistributive economy can appear quite like any other, simpler, reciprocal exchange. The difference, however, is that in the redistributive economy there exists a clear and systematic hierarchical relationship between the giver and receiver of the gift. In the purely reciprocal economy of the egalitarian tribe, the function of the exchange is to enable the sharing of risk amongst group members: A may have experienced a period of bad luck, but meanwhile B has been lucky and achieved a surplus of a necessary good. B exchanges the surplus on condition that, when the tables are turned, A will reciprocate. This ensures both that neither A nor B become significantly richer than one another, and also that – so long as both A and B do not simultaneously come upon hard times – neither experiences such material privation as to endanger life. 
When the relationship between giver and receiver is unequal, however, the giving and receiving of gifts can have another function: to make the poorer member a debtor and the richer member a creditor, thus further reinforcing the unequal structure of the relationship to the advantage of the richer member. In providing a gift to a subordinate member of such great value that the subordinate will never be able to repay, the superordinate ensures that their social structural advantage becomes permanent. By having ‘in his pockets’ a large number of indebted subordinates, the superordinate may then be able to benefit from a kind of obligatory economy of scale, such that subordinates, in an attempt to pay off their obligation, relinquish goods and perform services in exchange for lower returns than they would demand of someone of equal stature. The superordinate can then call upon the cheap pool of resources the debtors provide in order to ensnare further poor members in a trap of chronic debt. 

Within sociology and anthropology, this type of practice has been well chronicled by both Emile Durkheim’s nephew, Marcel Mauss, and by Pierre Bourdieu. Both Mauss and Bourdieu emphasised the importance of understanding the *relational* characteristics of social groups in understanding sociological phenomena, and their anthropological research – in Mauss’ case within *The Gift* {Mauss, 2000 #16}[Ref] and in Bourdieu’s case within his *Outline of a Theory of Practice* {Bourdieu, 1977 #17} and *Logic of Practice {Bourdieu, 1990 #18} *[Ref] -  have both detailed methods through which social inequality becomes reinforced through the practice of gift-giving. Bourdieu used the term ‘symbolic violence’ to refer to inequality-reinforcing ensnaring social logic behind the *prima facia *benevolent activity of gift-giving from higher to lower tiers of hierarchical members. Mauss used a Northern Native American term ‘potlatch’ to both epitomise and refer to a variety of practices and complex ceremonies of frenzied gift exchange between tribal members which, though apparently benevolent and spontaneous, were in fact calculated and competitive attempts to ensnare subordinates and beat rivals in a battle to assert power and authority over others. [Ref? Quote?]

Though these types of economic exchanges might be thought of as ‘reciprocal’, and the groups involved may be described as ‘tribes’, within Diamond’s typology these would instead be examples of *redistributive* economic systems within *chiefdoms. *Difference in terminology should not obscure the fact that these classical sociological and anthropological accounts complement rather than contradict Diamond’s own descriptions.
The Growth of Inequality
As the society becomes more complex the level of inequality within society tends to increase: a few individuals come to have disproportionate material, power and influence over everyone else. One reason for this, which is often overlooked because of its apparent banality, is that in very simple societies there are simply not enough surplus resources for it to be possible for some individuals to subsist off others.  A surplus of food is required in order for there to be any significant form of specialisation within a society. As well as technical specialists, including craftspeople and inventors, a surplus of food also allows military specialists – a dedicated army and police force – and administrative specialists to emerge. Diamond suggests that one way in which elites can maintain their positions of power over other members of society is to placate commoners by effectively serving the function of administrative specialists. This requires finding solutions to a variety of problems which result from the larger populations of the society. These include:

The problem of greater population density.

Large societies of food producers have not only more members but also higher population densities than do small bands of hunter-gatherers. … As population densities increases, the territory of that band-sized population of a few dozen would shrink to a small area, with more and more of life’s necessities having to be obtained outside the area. … [S]patial realities require that densely population regions support large and complexly organized societies. {Diamond, 1998 #14} [pp. 287-8]
To some extent, all other problems derive from this problem. The only way of ensuring that the environment can sustain large numbers of people is to reshape the environment towards human needs. This, in essence, is what the growth of agriculture and animal husbandry represent, and the causes and effects of this transition are, more so than anything else, the main issues discussed within Guns, Germs and Steel.
Unlike hunter-gathering, where tribes can be nomadic, moving from place to place as particular habitats start to show the strain of sustaining human life, agriculture requires people become sedentary. The artificial environments people build around their own particular needs – which includes monocultural fields of wheat as much as cities and other urban landscapes -  exist only where people create them. These artificial environments allow people to survive at greater rates, and in larger numbers, but only when they are situated nearby. 

Initially, the problem of excess population density could be alleviated by expansion: sub-sections of the population move from the ‘urban’ centre to the ‘rural’ periphery. But if a higher density, ‘urban’, social group then decides it wants the land on which the ‘rural’ periphery currently live, then the force of numbers is on the side the larger group. 

And what happens when there is nowhere left for the groups, wishing to avoid the associated with higher density living, to go to?
As recently as A.D. 1500, less than 20 percent of the world’s land area was marked off by boundaries into states run by bureaucrats and governed by laws. Today, all land except Antarctica’s is so divided. {Diamond, 1998 #14}[p. 266]

The problem of conflict between unrelated strangers.

To many people in modern societies, to suggest this could be a major problem is to betray an unreasonably and unrealistically cynical view about the human race. However, there does not appear to be sufficient evidence to be convinced that the problems of anarchic, reciprocal, internecine – and overwhelmingly male-on-male – killing between rivals and strangers can easily be severe enough that large population densities cannot be maintained unless the problem is dealt with. 
There is no hard-and-fast limit to the number at which non-elite solutions to the problem of lethal conflict cease to be effective, however: 
“a few hundred” seems to be an upper limit for group size compatible with everyone’s knowing everybody. … One reason why the organization of human government tends to change from that of a tribe to that of a chiefdom in societies with more than a few hundred members is that the difficult issue of conflict resolution between strangers becomes increasingly acute in larger groups. A fact further diffusing potential problems of conflict resolution in tribes is that almost everyone is related to everyone else, by blood or marriage or both. Those ties of relationships binding all tribal members make police, laws, and other conflict-resolving institutions of larger societies unnecessary, since any two villagers getting into an argument will share many kin, who apply pressure on them to keep it from becoming violent.  {Diamond, 1998 #14}[p. 271]
However, the “problem grows astronomically as the number of people making up the society increases.” {Diamond, 1998 #14}[p. 286]
Relationships within a band of 20 people involve only 190 two-person interactions (20 people times 19 divided by 2), but a band of 2,000 people would have 1,999,000 dyads. Each of those dyads represents a potential time bomb that could explode in a murderous argument. Each murder in band and tribal societies usually leads to an attempted revenge killing, starting one more unending cycle of murder and countermurder that destabilizes the society. {Diamond, 1998 #14}[p. 286]
The ‘solution’ elites have ‘offered’ to this problem is Hobbesian: “for one person, the chief, to exercise a monopoly on the legitimate use of force.”{Diamond, 1998 #14}[p. 273]
[The] chief held a recognized office, filled by hereditary right. Instead of the decentralized anarchy of a village meeting, the chief was a permanent centralized authority, made all significant decisions, and had a monopoly on critical information. {Diamond, 1998 #14}[p.273]
It is worth taking note, at this point, that the words ‘police’, ‘polity’, ‘policy’, ‘politic’, ‘political’ and ‘polite’ all share the same etymological lineage. 

The problem of transferring goods between members.

One of the primary advantages of any form of social group is the ability it provides its members to share risks. People are simply not utility-maximising robots, but instead risk-minimising, mortal, animals. The gain to one’s well-being of having twice as much food to eat as one needs is not twice as beneficial as having half as much food as one needs to eat is detrimental: the former provides some slight gluttonous pleasure; the latter brings the chronic, intense pain of starvation. Though one can calculate the expected return from an investment on average, what matters most to people is the minimisation of the probability that one’s return will ever fall below some minimal threshold: the threshold required for survival. In small groups, where everyone knows each other by name and reputation, sharing one’s surplus ensures that one’s own fortunes are always closely aligned to those of the group, and thus that, so long as the group’s resources per capita are above the starvation threshold, no one will starve. 
However, as the group size is small, it may be that none or very few of the members have been fortunate in acquiring resources, and thus bad fortune could mean the starvation of the entire group. Having a larger group means greater stability in the per capita wealth of the group; however, this larger size also leads to far greater logistical difficulties in ensuring effective distribution of such wealth to all members. 
As Diamond puts it:      
Any society requires means to transfer goods between its members. One individual may happen to acquire more of some essential commodity on one day and less on another. Because individuals have different talents, one individual consistently tends to wind up with an excess of some essentials and deficit of others. In small societies with a few pairs of members, the resulting necessary transfers of goods can be arranged directly between pairs of individuals or families, by reciprocal exchanges. But  the same mathematics that make direct pairwise conflict resolution inefficient in large societies makes direct pairwise economic transfers also inefficient. Large societies can function economically only if they have a redistributive economy in addition to a reciprocal economy. Goods in excess of an individual’s needs must be transferred from the individual to a centralized authority, which then redistributes the goods to individuals with deficits. {Diamond, 1998 #14}[p. 287]
As well as, and to an extent despite, the symbolic violence of the potlatch, the ceding of some proportion of one’s wealth to an administrative elite may be tolerated by commoners so long as the administrators ensure the availability of sufficient food on occasions when both oneself, and one’s friends, are simultaneously suffering from acute shortage. The establishment of the administrated redistributive economy, in parallel with the kinship-based reciprocal economy, ensures that members of larger social groups are able to access its risk-minimising benefits. 

The growing impossibility of communal decision making.

[Write something appropriate here…]
Elites, the Establishment, and Durable Inequality
[Introduction]
[C]hiefdoms introduced the dilemma fundamental to all centrally governed, nonegalitarian societies. At best , they do so by providing expensive services impossible to contract for on an individual basis. At worst, they function unabashedly as kleptocracies, transferring net wealth from commoners to upper classes. These noble and selfish functions are inextricably linked, although some governments emphasize much more of one function than of the other. The difference between a kleptocrat and a wise statesman, between a robber baron and a public benefactor, is merely one of degree: a matter of just how large a percentage of the tribute extracted from producers is retained by the elite, and how much the commoners like the public uses to which the redistributed tribute is put. {Diamond, 1998 #14}[p. 276]
[Write something]
What should an elite do to gain popular support while still maintaining a more comfortable lifestyle than commoners? Kleptocrats throughout the ages have resorted to a mixture of four solutions:

Disarm the populace, and arm the elite. […]
Make the masses happy by redistributing much of the tribute received, in popular ways. […]
Use the monopoly of force to promote happiness, by maintaining public order and curbing violence. […]
[C]onstruct an ideology or religion justifying kleptocracy {Diamond, 1998 #14}[p. 277]

[Write something]
When supernatural beliefs gained [the function of serving to justify central authority, justify transfer of wealth, or maintaining peace between unrelated individuals] and became institutionalized, they were transformed into what we term a religion. {Diamond, 1998 #14}[pp. 276-7]
Diamond Quotes
A prerequisite for living in settlements is either food production or else a productive environment with especially concentrated resources that can be hunted and gathered within a small area.{Diamond, 1998 #14} [p. 271]
Tribes … share with bands an “egalitarian” social system, without ranked lineages or classes. Not only is status not inherited; no member of a traditional tribe or band can become disproportionately wealthier by his or her own efforts, because each individual has debts and obligations to many others.{Diamond, 1998 #14} [p.272]
As regards population size, chiefdoms were considerably larger than tribes, ranging from several thousand to several tens of thousands of people. That size created serious potential for internal conflict because, for any person living in a chiefdom, the vast majority of other people in the chiefdom were neither closely related by blood or marriage nor known by name. With the rise of chiefdoms around 7,500 years ago, people had to learn,  for the first time in history, how to encounter strangers regularly without attempting to kill them.{Diamond, 1998 #14}
 [p.273]
[W]hy do the commoners tolerate the transfer of the fruits of their hard labor to kleptocrats? This question, raised by political theorists from Plato to Marx, is raised anew by voters in every modern election. Kleptocracies with little public support run the risk of being overthrown, wither by downtrodden commoners or by upstart would-be replacement kleptocrats seeking public support by promising a higher ratio of services rendered to fruits stolen. {Diamond, 1998 #14} [p. 276]
When a large portion of the goods received from commoners  was not redistributed to them but was retained and consumed by the chiefly lineages and craftspeople, the redistribution became tribute, a precursor of taxes that made its first appearance in chiefdoms. From the commoners the chiefs claimed not only goods but also labor for construction of public works, which again might return to benefit the commoners (for example, irrigation systems to help feed everybody) or instead benefited mainly the chiefs (for instance, lavish tombs). {Diamond, 1998 #14}[p. 275]

title: House and Home
text:
House and Home
[Big idea: A home is an asset with a variety of forms of potential value. It has value as a physiological good, which satiates low-level needs for warmth and shelter. It has value as a social (status) good, which displays to others one’s place within society. And it also has value as a speculative good: just as someone might pay more for some company shares than they ever assume they’ll recoup from dividend payments over a ten-or-twenty year periods, simply because they believe other traders will payeven morefor the asset (presumably because they also believe others will payyet more stillfor the asset), so someone might pay more to own a house than they ever assume they’ll be able to save from not renting a house for twenty to thirty years, if they think they can sell the house for more than they bought it for. Chaos and complexity theory suggest that speculative goods change in their value in a fashion which is inherently unpredictable in some senses, but also which follows a predictable signature. They enter what has been termed a ‘metastable’ region, where, though it is known they are susceptible to sudden changes in value in a known direction,whenthat sudden change in value will happen is extremely unpredictable. This quality of pattern is the subject of investigation of a branch of mathematical physics known as ‘catastrophe theory’.] [Homes are also valuable as collateral, which is a bit like saying home are valuable because they’re valuable. More creditors are willing to provide their services, at more favourable rates, if the debtor has a value asset against which the potential cost of the creditors service may be secured. This is one of many mechanisms by which ‘rich-get-richer’ dynamics can emerge in asset holdings. ] [Homes also help to cement social inequalities through their differing access to good schools. Children are no different from other age groups in the extent to which they’re more interested by their own peers than other peers. This means they’re more interested in the values and opinions of their fellow schoolchildren than of their parents or grandparents. It also means that the best way to improve the chances of a school child doing well at school is to be educated within a school where the school peer group’s social norm is to do well at school. There’s almost certainly a strong autocorrelation between factors which leads to a ‘lock-in’ effect – persons get a good job because they got good qualifications because they went to a good school, this allows them to move into an expensive house near a good school, which their children go to, who repeat the cycle in the next generation. The converse is also true: bad schooling in generation x leads to bad schooling in generation x+1. Without the financial resources to live in houses in expensive areas, the children don’t get to go to good schools, and don’t get good grades, and so the cycle repeats in the next generation.

title: Chaos and Complexity
text:
Chaos and Complexity The terms ‘chaotic’, ‘complex’ and ‘complicated’, often seem to be used interchangeably, and so I believe it would be worthwhile for me to explain how I shall be using the terms, and what the differences between them are. I’ll try to offer, where available, citations and ‘authoritative’ arguments in favour of the definitions I’m using, but where I cannot one should just try to accept that sticking to any one consistent meaning for a term is generally better than not. Words are, after all, only meaningful through common social convention.
Chaotic: A phenomena is chaotic where some endogenous components of the system which characterise it exhibit positive feedback and apparent randomness. From a physicalist perspective, it might be considered that, in principle, all phenomena are perfectly predictable, as all comprise entirely of deterministic causal relationships. However, due to practical limits on the amount of information which can be collected and fed into a model designed to predict the phenomena, the most practicable model one could employ would be stochastic rather than deterministic. A stochastic model might be considered a general framework which could encompass both completely random and completely non-random (deterministic) phenomena, employing such a model in either extreme case is only of conceptual rather than practical importance. A stochastic model includes at least one probabilistic function. A probabilistic function differs from a deterministic function in that, unlike with a deterministic function, performing the same operation on a deterministic function may yield a different result. Understanding what this means could be helped with the following thought experiment:
Imagine a black box with two buttons and two lights. The buttons are labelled “Left” and “Right”, and the lights are labelled “Red” and “Blue” (for the simple reason that one of the buttons is on the left, and the other on the right; and that one of the lights produces red light, and the other produces blue light. The actual labels don’t matter. They merely help people to identify the different parts – the lights and the buttons – more easily.) If one presses the button on the left (called “Left”), the red light turns on. When the button in the right (“Right”) is pressed, the blue light turns on. When neither of the buttons are pressed, neither of the lights are on. When both of the buttons are pressed, both of the lights are on. In this case, each of the things one could do to the inputs leads to a particular thing happening to the lights. The black box is said to ‘map’ each of the possible combinations of button-pressing to a particular combination of light-illuminating. The black box is said tofunctionas a system which converts ‘inputs’ (the buttons) to ‘outputs’ (the lights). It is said to be deterministic because the same combination of inputsalwaysleads to the same combination of outputs.
Consider another black box, which looks exactly the same as the one just described. This time, however, a particular input does not always lead to a particular output. “Left” does not always cause ‘Red”, and “Right” does not always cause “Blue”. Instead, a particular input leads may lead to a range of possible outputs: Pressing Left on one particular occasion leads to Red, another time it leads to Blue, another time it may lead to both Red and Blue at the same time, and another time it leads to neither Red nor Blue. This black box, unlike the previous one, is not deterministic. Understanding this second black box requires doing the following: choose a particular input (Red or Blue individually, or both Red and Blue at the same time), and press it, observing what output this leads to. Then do this again, and again, and again… Continue doings this – pressing a particular input and observing the output – all the while recording what outputs one gets from the input, a large (but known) number of times. Now, with the data one has just gathered from performing this simple experiment on the box, draw a histogram – a visual tally – of the number of times the particular input has led to each of the possible outputs. This histogram has a particular shape to it. This shape can be used to form a formal description of how the given input relates to the range of possible outputs. The formal description of how the given input relates to the range of outputs is known as aprobability function, and possesses some conceptual subtlety which is important to understand: It is a way of mapping the given input to the range of outputs, rather than just a single output. By performing this kind of experiment for each of the input combinations, a probability function can be constructed for each input combination, which defines how that particular input combination relates to any and all of the output combinations. As long as one assumes that nothing inside the black box has changed over the course of the experiments performed, and that the future will be like the past (i.e. that one is not wrong to use inductive reasoning), then one can use the probability functions one has created to try to predict what the outcome of a particular input combination will be. The prediction makes no intuitive sense for a single ‘trial’ of the system (i.e. a single ‘press’ of a particular combination of buttons), but does make some sense when talking about a collection of ‘trials’. The type of prediction one might make from the probability function might be along the lines of: “If I were to press the red button five times, I would expect to see the red light alone turn on three times, the blue light to turn one time, and both lights to turn on one time. I don’t know, however, the in which these output events will occur, however”. (If one then pressed the button five times, one might or might not see the tally one has not predicted. If, however, one performs multiple five-trial ‘trials’ (i.e. one performs a trial of trials), and finds an average of the number of trials [….]
This subsectioned has segued into a strange, brave and foolish attempt to describe the concepts behind functions and probability!. I’m bored! I think I’ve demonstrated why mathematical formulae aren’t just difficult to understand on purpose: they are ways of formally describing things which are difficult to describe verbally, as I have been trying to do just here!

title: Work and Worklessness
text:
Work and Worklessness
[My overall aim in this section is to promote the idea that work is ultimately one of a number of possible ways an individual could demonstrate to members of their community that they’re ‘worthy’ members. If they don’t feel obliged by themselves and their peers to work then they’re less likely to. The elderly don’t feel obliged because the general heuristic assumption is that they’ve already contributed to the work of others and so their social standing is ‘in the bank’ (so to speak). Unemployed in areas of high unemployment don’t feel obliged to work because most of their peers don’t work either, so their social standing relative to their peers is OK. Within such communities, it would make sense for a certain insularity and cultural myopia to set in, as considering impossibly more affluent groups would be deleterious to one’s well-being.]
[Work is a way to acquire money. Money maps onto a simple logic of practice of reciprocation and obligation. Bourdieu noted that tribal members oftenfearedreceiving gifts from other tribal members, as this meant they became what in modern societies would be classed as a ‘debtor’. Polanyi – in the Great Transformation – gives an interesting description of previous forms of social reciprocation. Capital is both something that’s (possibly) an extension of such logics of practice, and something that’s qualitatively different. Steven Pinker wrote something similar, but of course he’s not popular amongst conventional social sciences, so should probably not be referenced favourably/authoritatively!]
According to the biogeographer Jared Diamond (1991), the average numbers of hours worked by members of modern capitalist civilisations tends to be much in excess of those worked by members of hunter-gatherer societies. This observation is, I believe, non-trivial in helping one to conceptualise the ontology of work.
The labour economist Andrew Clark has written a number of papers exploring the link between wellbeing and employment, whose findings bear considering in some detail. In the paper, “Unemployment as a social norm”, Clark (2003) uses the British Household Panel Survey to assess the effect unemployment has on an individual’s sense of wellbeing, given the employment status of the individual’s peers. This leads to some interesting findings. According to Clark, though “well-being is typically negatively correlated with others’ employment … a closer examination reveals a distinct pattern … : the well-being of the employed is often lower when the unemployment rate of others is higher; on the contrary, the unemployed report higher levels of well-being as others’ unemployment rises.” “Loosely speaking,“ writes Clark, “unemployment hurts less the of there is of it around”(2003: 325-6).
There are, broadly speaking, two types of poverty: absolute (privation) and relative (deprivation). Both have deleterious effects upon health, but through different pathways. For the vast majority of human history, poverty has been absolute, and absolute poverty is Darwinian: absolute poverty is the absence of sufficient resources to stay alive. Absolute poverty, in the West, thankfully no longer exists. But this does not mean that poverty no longer exists, and no longer kills. In the Victorian age, the poor were liable to be rakishly thin, and die from malnutrition and preventable disease. Now, the poor are liable to be overweight, and die from malnutrition and preventable disease. Whereas before the poor died from a lack of calories, now they die of an excess of calories. Whereas before they died of tuberculosis and dysentery, now they die of liver, lung, and heart disease.
According to the social epidemiologist Richard Wilkinson, the pathway through which relative poverty kills is social stress. Wilkinson’s ‘relative deprivation hypothesis’ has been the kernel for a lot of academic research, and is consonant with the approach adopted by poverty researchers such as Townsend, who made poverty a measure of whether someone can afford to have something that the populace at large believes everyone should be able to have.
[There’s an interesting article by Jonathan Bradshaw which compares different measures of poverty. Amongst the elderly relative measures of poverty were less well correlated with psychological poverty – ‘feeling poor’ – than amongst other groups. I want to clarify this and integrate it somehow with the norm-formation theory – the idea that people form most of their habits when they’re in their teens/early 20s (hence advertisers pumping more money into targeting the young – who are more impressionable – rather than the middle-aged and old – who have more purchasing power). An explanation for the breakdown in correlation between psychological and relative measures of poverty amongst the elderly is that the habits of the elderly were formed at a time when the average level of material wealth was much less than at present – link to GDP growth – and because the norm-forming window has passed them long ago the elderly don’t consider not having as much as the average person to be that much of a problem.
[Similarly strange findings amongst the elderly emerged in Dorling’s Japanese regional inequality study. These are discussed briefly in Wilkinson’s very recent paper summarising the results of over 150 studies which assess the relative deprivation hypothesis. ]
[I read an interesting article in the FT (around November 2004) defining the upper-middle-class/London poverty line… at well above the national average. C.f. George Orwell’s description of his family’s socioeconomic status as ‘lower upper middle class’: the ‘poverty line’ for a family who sends their children to a top boarding school!]
[Of course, Veblen is relevant here. More generally I want to try to translate from the conceptual to the operational and find out more about the theory of ‘Veblen Goods’ – goods whose demandincreasesrather than decreases as price increases – and how it relates to poverty. Richard King – my previous supervisor – lent me his copy of Hirsh’s Social Limits to Growth(?) which is also worth mentioning]
[E.O. Wilson wrote about a ‘ratchet’ effect with respect to human technology and growth – that one can move ‘up’ but not ‘down’ – which is interesting and conceptually relevant… but unfortunately Wilson tends to be characterised by sociologists as a cloven-hoofed biological determinist, so referencing him directly probably wouldn’t be wise.]
Get exact numbers and references: from memory think numbers are about 1/2: ~18 in h-g societies vs. ~36 in modern societies Reference Clarify Reference Clarify/define more exactly Reference: DJ Taylor’s biography of Orwell Add to bibliography Conscilience

title: Chapter 2: Authorities and Bureaucracies
text:
Chapter Two: Authorities and Bureaucracies
Introduction
Chapter One presented a micro-level theory of decision making within individuals, and a macro-level theory of the emergence of complex, stratified societies. This chapter will use these two strands – micro-level behaviours, and macro-level social structures – and attempt to interweave them, in order to present a coherent theory of the emergence and functioning of formalised, hierarchical, bureaucratic social organisations.
As with chapter one, this chapter will proceed mainly through a relatively focused exegesis of a small number of existing theories, rather than through a more expansive but less detailed review of contemporary research within the given field of enquiry. There are a number of reasons for this approach: firstly, to present a sufficient discussion of a particular theory about a given social process such that, even without prior knowledge of the theory, the reader will be able to understand the core aspects of the theory such that the manner in which they will be applied is relatively transparent and their appropriateness (or otherwise) does not simply have to be ‘taken on faith’; second; secondly, to reduce the risk of accidental ‘ventriloquism’ of the original theorists and fallacious misinterpretation of their ideas; and thirdly, to improve readability through greater narrative consistency, and by reducing the risk of obscuring key points through continual digression to discuss counterpoints. 

Also, as with chapter one, this chapter will focus on two well-known theorists: in this case, Max Weber and Stanley Milgram. As with chapter one, one theorist (Weber) will be utilised to adopt a ‘macro-level’ position and the other (Milgram) will be used to present a ‘micro-level’ position. However, unlike in chapter one, by the end of this chapter the connection between the two theorists will be made explicit: a ‘mid-level’ theory will be presented which suggests how the micro-level observations of Milgram corroborate with macro-level theories of Weber. The micro-level theory presented in this chapter operates at a ‘larger scale’ than (‘is “macro” with respect to’) the micro-level theory presented in chapter one; conversely, the macro-level theory presented within this chapter is at a ‘lower level’ than (‘is “micro” with respect to’) the macro-level theory presented within chapter one. From the perspective of chapter one, therefore, this second chapter represents a partial convergence towards mid-level theorisation, as the following schematic illustrates:
Figure 1: Schematic suggesting how Chapters One and Two relate
Jared Diamond (Macro)
Max Weber (Macro)
Stanley Milgram (Micro)
Antonio Damasio (Micro)
Convergent Theory (Meso)
Chapter One
Chapter Two
This ‘convergent theory’ will combine Max Weber’s discussions regarding the machine-like nature of organizations, with Milgram’s theory of the agentic mode: a sudden switching in individuals’ form of behaviour, in specific and well-defined circumstances. The convergent theory will suggest that the bureaucratic machine exists largely because of the extent to which individuals have a tendency to adopt agentic, rather than autonomous, modes of behaviour when situated within formal and hierarchical social structures. [write some more]
Weber
Introducing Modernity
Max Weber, together with Karl Marx and Emile Durkheim, is commonly taught as one of the three principal representatives of ‘classical sociology’. Sociology emerged in Europe in the nineteenth century as a mode of enquiry directed, broadly, at addressing the social conditions and consequences of ‘modernity’. 
There is a danger in the previous statement of leaving the term ‘modernity’ – like any other abstract noun – undefined. As the aim of this discussion is not to needlessly introduce terms and then leave them semantically empty, it may be worth presenting a short, heuristic, checklist of features which characterise ‘modernity’.
Modernity refers to a qualitatively different form of social structure and government to historically prior forms. More specifically, various forms of new technology allow ‘elites’ – operating through many levels of hierarchical command - to exert increasingly direct rule over large populations of ‘commoners’. This differs from the previous form of rule by the highest strata of elites, which tended to be more indirect, mediated through lower tiers of semi-autonomous elites who were more geographically proximate to those over whom they ruled. Indirect rule by the highest tier of elites meant granting significant governmental autonomy to lower tiers of elites, and thus relied upon a relatively complex and interdependent tapestry of power structures tapering to a single point. This mutual interdependence – between lower and higher tiers of the power structure – meant the level of direct influence the highest tier could have upon lower tiers was relatively limited: the power of the higher tiers was heavily contingent upon the willingness of lower tier elites to accept such rule, and if superior elite dissatisfied many of the subordinate elites then the subordinates could be liable to depose the superior, and either promote one of the members from their own ranks to the rank held by the usurped, or to the dispose of that higher rank completely, and fragment into a number of autonomous regional polities. Consequently, the level of administrative and cultural heterogeneity was relatively large: ‘commoners’ in different geographic regions thus tended to be subject to widely different social conditions, speak in very different – even mutually comprehensible – dialects, and had social identities which were, primarily, familial and local to the relatively small space of land on which they lived. 
Integral to the transition from the feudal, indirect rule and social conditions of pre-modernity, to the more centralised, direct rule of modernity, then, was the emergence of the nation state and nationalism as, respectively, effective systems of geographically distal but direct rule, and increasing homogeneity of social identity within the geographic region in which the nation state operates. Although states have arguably existed for millennia, like the city-states of ancient Greece, such states functioned, over large areas, primarily through indirect rather than direct rule.  Within Europe, the continent within which ‘classical sociology’ emerged,  the ‘state-based nationalism’ of geographically extended direct rule only “became widely available, or even technically feasible … during the nineteenth and twentieth centuries”.   [1][ p. 175] 
Weber and Modernity
Within the above broad-brush definition and description of modernity, the focus of the author’s attention has been on the transition from conditions of indirect to direct state rule, and the co-emergence of state-level social identities. Sociology emerged, to an extent, in the effort to explain these transitions and their consequences, but also emerged, to an extent, because of  many of the changes it sought to explain: faster, more widespread communication and travel technology, which allowed greater intra-and-international collaboration between luminaries; more efficient agricultural technology, and efficient administration of foodstuffs and other necessities, which allowed increased specialisation and divisions-of-labour, including more academic specialists. 
One of the parts of the puzzle of modernity which Weber attempted to explain was with respect to these systems and mechanisms of direct rule: these were, and are, *bureaucracies,* * *and his analysis of these forms of social organisation stressed their ‘machine-like’ qualities. 
In order to understand Weber’s analysis of bureaucracies further, the author will now present a sequence of passages  from Weber’s posthumously published Economy and Society [Ref, confirm], together with the author’s own reflections and interpretations of these passages within the given context.
Modern officialdom functions in the following specific manner:

There is a principle of fixed and official jurisdictial areas, which are generally ordered by rules, that is, by laws or administrative regulations. 


The regular activities required for the purposes of the bureaucratically governed structure are distributed in a fixed way as official duties.
The authority to give the commands required for the discharge of these duties is distributed in a stable way and is strictly delimited by rules concerning the coercive means, physical, sacerdotal, or otherwise, which may be placed at the disposal of officials.
Methodical provision is made for the regular and continuous fulfilment of these duties and for the execution of the corresponding rights; only persons who have generally regulated qualifications to serve are employed.

In public and lawful government these three elements constituted “bureaucratic authority”. In private economic domination, they constituted bureaucratic “management”. Bureaucracy, thus understood, is fully developed in political and ecclesiastical communities only in the modern state, and, in the private economy, only in the most advanced institutions of capitalism. [2][p. 104]
Thus, Weber suggests that bureaucracies are specific neither to public- nor private-sphere social structures. Here it may be worth reminding ourselves of the historical contingency behind the public-private sphere distinctions which have been made. Thinking back to the second part of the first chapter, it is a general quality of more complex societies - with greater population densities and more efficient food production than sparser, simpler societies - that they show a trend towards increased specialisation of roles: as food becomes easier to produce, larger proportions of the population are able to engage in activities other than subsistence agriculture. The transition from subsistence agriculture to other forms of activity is gradual, and by no means inevitable. When just a small proportion of a population, for a small proportion of its time, is able to engage in non-subsistence activities, such non-subsistence activity tends to be generalist, and ‘political’ (related to the manner in which elites stay in power) in nature. The emergence of more specialisation of roles – for example with a proto-political, general ‘militia’ role splitting into separate redistributive, medical, public health, police and army roles – occurs ‘later’ in the process; and the emergence of such a large population not engaged in  subsistence activities  that the even non-governmental specialists begin to exhibit complex organisational structures and require their own, separate, administrative specialists, occurs even ‘later’ still.   
As Weber suggests:
Permanent and public office authority, with fixed jurisdiction, is not the historical rule but rather the exception. This is so even in large political structures such as those of the ancient Orient, the Germanic and Mongolian empires of conquest, or of many feudal structures of state. In all these cases, the ruler executes the most important measures through personal trustees, table-companions, or court-servants. Their commissions and authority are not precisely delimited and are temporarily called into being for each case. [2] [p. 105]
Here the distinction is being made between polities which operate through the familial or ‘patrimonial’ systems of indirect rule, and the more direct rule of modernist nation-states.

The principles of office hierarchy and of levels of graded authority mean a firmly ordered system of super-and subordination in which there is a supervision of the lower offices by the higher ones. Such a system offers the governed the possibility of appealing the decision of a lower office to its higher authority, in a definitely regulated manner. With the full development of the bureaucratic type, the office hierarchy is monocratically organized. The principle of hierarchical office authority is found in all bureaucratic structures: in state and ecclesiastical structures as well as in large party organizations and private enterprises. It does not matter for the character of bureaucracy whether its authority is called “private” or “public”. [2][p. 105]

Two important points are made here: firstly, that the social structure of bureaucracies is strictly hierarchical: a single subordinate does not report to multiple superiors, but instead, in general, there is a single vertical chain of command by which superiors are connected to subordinates. This ‘tree-like’ structure is one that we tend to be so familiar with, for example through official organisational charts, that it is worth emphasising that these are far from a ubiquitous form of social structure. [Network analysis; friendship groups; small-world theory – Milgram and Grosvenetter (sic?!) ] Secondly, the point is reiterated that the existence of a bureaucratic social structure can and does occur both within organisations whose intended functions relate to the public and the private sphere.
When the principle of jurisdictional “competency” is fully carried through, hierarchical subordination – at least in public office –does not meant [sic] that the “higher” authority is simply authorized to take over the business from the “lower”. Indeed, the opposite is the rule. Once established and having fulfilled its task, an office tends to continue in existence and be held by another incumbent. [2][p. 105]
A subtle though important point – and implicitly a distinction from other forms of social structure – is made here: the official roles that individuals within bureaucracies occupy are ontologically distinct from the individuals themselves. Different individuals come to occupy the same bureaucratic roles, and through this the role comes to become an obdurate and non-personal feature of the bureaucratic organisation as a whole. 
Though, given our present historical conditions and experiences, the above may now appear to be stating the obvious, one should not discount how the emergence and strengthening of this distinction – between the individual and the functional role the individual occupies within the bureaucracy – marked a pronounced change in the quality of social relations. 
Two questions are worth considering at this point: firstly, how do individuals come to perform the roles required of them by bureaucracies; or to state it another way, how individuals come to behave effectively and obediently, as functionaries within bureaucratic systems? Secondly, how to these roles come to have their ontologically obdurate and distinct character, different to, and often lasting beyond, those of the individuals who perform them? 
Appropriate responses to these questions are somewhat intertwined, but the author believes that Milgram’s theory of – and very substantial evidence for – the agentic mode does much to answer the first question. 
As regards the second question:   

The management of the modern office is based upon written documents (“the files”), which are preserved in their original or draught form. There is, therefore, a staff of subaltern officials and scribes of all sorts. The body of officials actively engage in a “public” office, along with the respective apparatus of material implements and the files, make up a “bureau.” In private enterprise, “the bureau” is often called “the office.”[2] [p. 105]

Here, it becomes apparent that literacy and writing represent a substantial part of the enabling technologies of rational, direct, bureaucratic rule, doing much to enable the obdurate character of bureaucratic roles to emerge. Whereas, over time, personal memories become distorted, a written document does not. So long as the behaviours and duties expected of an individual who fulfils a role are specified clearly enough within written documents, then the character of the role will change little irrespective of the individual performer. [Heuristic; obduracy of social roles through generations etc…] 
In principle, the modern organization of the civil service separates the bureau from the private domicile of the official, and, in general, bureaucracy segregates official activity as something distinct from the sphere of private life. Public monies and equipment are divorced from the private property of the official. This condition is everywhere the product of a long development. Nowadays, it is found in public as well as in private enterprises; in the latter, the principle extends even to the leading entrepreneur. In principle, the executive office is separated from the household, business from private correspondence, and business assets from private fortunes. The more consistency the modern type of business management has been carried through the more are these separations the case. The beginnings of this process are to be found as early as the Middle ages.[2] [p. 105-6]
Again, the extent to which the organisational role is made distinct from the individual performing it is emphasised.

Office management, at least all specialized office management – and such management is distinctly modern – usually presupposes thorough and expert training. This increasingly holds for the modern executive and employee of private enterprises, in the same manner as it holds for the state official.[2] [p. 106]

Here, it is suggested that more extensive and specific education, in addition to those required for simple literacy, is also an enabling technology, allowing individuals to adopt specified roles more effectively.

When the office is fully developed, official activity demands the full working capacity of the official, irrespective of the fact that his obligatory time in the bureau may be firmly delimited. In the normal case, this is only the produce of a long development, in the public as well as in the private office. Formally, in all cases, the normal state of affairs was reversed: official business was discharged as a secondary activity. [2][p. 106]


The management of the office follows general rules, which are more or less stable, more or less exhaustive, and which can be learned. Knowledge of these rules represent a special technical learning which the official possesses. In involved jurisprudence, or administrative or business management. [2] [p. 106]

The function of jurisprudence should not be underestimated. The extent to which law exists as a enabling technology of direct rule will be considered later within the chapter, but for now it simply needs to be noted that Weber, whose father was a lawyer and who studied law himself, was aware of this.
Weber concludes his discussion as follows:

The reduction of modern office management to rules is deeply embedded in its very nature. The theory of modern public administration, for instance, assumes that the authority to order certain matters by decree – which has been legally granted to public authorities – does not entitle the bureau to regulate the matter by commands given for each case, but only to regulate the matter abstractly. This stands in extreme contrast to the regulation of all relationships through individual privileges and bestowals of favour, which is absolutely dominant in patrimonialism, at least in so far as such relationships are not fixed by sacred tradition.[2] [pp. 106]
Within the above, one can distinguish between what might be referred to as enabling conditions of political modernity (The system of direct rule) and the distinctive qualities  of political modernity. 
The enabling conditions suggested include:

High levels of literacy amongst functionaries within bureaucracies. As the size of the bureaucratic structure increases, so the pool of literate individuals required to occupy roles within the bureaucracy rises. This would imply a relatively comprehensive system of primary and secondary education amongst a substantial proportion (though not necessarily a majority) of the population
Effective systems of teaching specialist, vocational skills required for consistent administration of power. [i.e. law]
Discretisation and specialisation of roles. [Write something]
No significant rivals to the dominant political power structure. [write something: mutual benefits and collaborations between public and private sphere interests: companies work /trade best when places are well governed]
Technologies for rapid communication. [electricity, roads, military pacification]

Dual Modes of Communication within Bureaucracies 
The enabling technologies of communication are very important to consider here. One should distinguish between two types of communication technology, both of which are required for the effective and long-term functioning of direct rule through bureaucracy: these are, to state it crudely, 
- slow and long-term 

- fast and short-term 
Fast-and-short-term technologies are required in order that specific, central commands from the top of the bureaucracy are quickly and effectively, with a minimum of distortion and delay, sent to and received by relevant subordinates a number of distal chains from the commanding authority. Without quick, short-term communication, the behaviours of lower subordinates would be difficult to control in any particular circumstances, and so the system of rule would be indirect rather than direct, relying on subordinates responding appropriately – and largely autonomously – if and when any crises emerge in their particular territory; likewise, subordinates in the power structure would not be as able to transmit urgent and useful information to the highest tiers at an appropriate speed, and thus would not be able to call on the resources the highest tiers has at its disposal – for example if there is some form of emergency within their particular territory - and thus would not benefit as much from their relationship with higher tiers in the power structure. 
A paradigmatic example of an enabling technology of fast-and-short-term communication within the nineteenth century is the development of electrical telegraphy, thus allowing commands and information, which would previously have taken days, weeks or months to reach from one territory to another, to now be transmitted in minutes. By the end of the nineteenth century, such technology traversed much of the globe, including line of telegraph cables which stretched across the Atlantic, thus allowing quick communication between the ‘old world’ and the ‘new world’.  One should note, however, that even something as revolutionary as electrical technology represents part of a (albeit relatively punctured) continuum of rapid-communication technologies: for example, well-maintained networks of roads and paths could be considered part of this continuum. The penchant of the Roman Empire for building long, straight roads which cross traverse long stretches of largely empty territory, connecting urban centres, could also be thought to be a response to the need for fast communications technologies over increasing distances (as messengers can deliver messages more quickly and reliably when they do not have to battle against undergrowth, continually re-assess and re-check their direction, and not know whether they should avoid or go to intermediate populated areas). Carrier pigeons could be considered another, slightly more obvious, example of this type of technology.
 With respect to fast-and-short-term communications, the above paragraph has suggested that the actual speed of such technologies can vary immensely, with the same information, taking between a few milliseconds (email) to a few months (road and sea) to transmit depending upon the technology available; in other words, fast can actually be very slow by current standards. However, the fundamental function of such technologies – as an enabler of a particular form of communication – remains the same. This distinction – between medium and intention of communication – should be borne in mind when considering how fast-and-short-term communication is distinct from slow-and-long-term communication: the slow-and-long-termness of this type of communication is inherent within its intent, its quality of communication; the medium in which the communication is enacted simply dictates the extent to which the technology effectively enables such communication.  

With the above in mind, one can now begin to differentiate the purposes of slow-and-long-term from fast-and-short-term. *Slow-and-long-term* *communication influences and shapes behaviours of individuals within roles in a manner that is obdurate and general, whereas fast-and-short-term communication influences behaviours in ways that are transitory and specific.* Job specifications, codes of practice, and legal documents are all examples of slow-and-long-term communication technologies applied within bureaucracies. *It is primarily through this form of communication that bureaucracies remain relatively consistent in their internal structure and functions, despite changes in the line-ups of individuals employed to perform such roles.* 
Organisations and Organisms
With respect to the above – distinguishing between these two forms of internal communication within command structures – it may be useful to remind ourselves of the concept of the organism discussed within the first part of chapter one. Within a complex organism such as a person, a distinction was made between more localised quick-and-short-term signalling mechanisms, which tends to be electrical in nature; and more generalised slow-and-long-term signalling mechanisms, which tend to be chemical in nature. Patterns and sequences of these more generalised signals, and the responses of sites within the organism to such signals, are emotions. These can take a relatively long time – a few seconds in some cases – to emerge, and have a relatively long-standing effect on the behaviour of the organism, perhaps lasting a few minutes or hours. Quick-and-short-term signalling travels much faster through the organism, and exacts an effect far more quickly (the removal of a hand from a hot stove takes a fraction of a second), but does not result in more long-term change to the behaviours of a larger aspect of the organism (unless it is converted into a long-term signal: a sudden burst of pain leading to the release of adrenaline, for example). Additionally, the organism learns a large number of dispositional memories of various ‘objects’ (in the broad, technical sense previously employed) that it encounters: these memories constitute learned patterns of behavioural response to given situations.     
Hopefully, by now, the extent to which the bureaucratic organisation might usefully be considered analogous to the biological organism are obvious: Formal rules, which accrete and emerge over time in response to new general classes of situation, or are emulated and adapted from other organisations, produce complex, specific, stereotyped patterns of response to a given, identified situation, and thus bear some qualitative similarity to the emotions and dispositional tendencies of organisms. Likewise, fast communication technology, such as email or telephone, allows the quick relaying of signals to or from a site of central command; the central authority can then make a quick, specific decisions with respect to a very local aspect of the organisation – for example telling the head of a particular department that their request for funding for a particular project has been granted or rejected; or the central authority may choose to make more general changes to the organisation: asking the legal or quality-control department to draft new codes of practice by which employees within the organisation as a whole should thereafter conform (analogous inducing an emotion or creating a new dispositional memory).

So long as one is aware of the extent to which the above represents *analogy* rather than exact *homology* of function, the author believes the above comparison between organisation and organism to be useful in clarifying *why, in bureaucracies, paperwork is such an integral feature of their general coherence and function.* In a very general, cybernetics or systems theory sense, it should certainly not be controversial to state that bureaucratic organisations, as well as biological organisations, appear to exhibit the qualities of being:

Bounded: with a clear, categorical demarcation between within  and without.
Self-regulating: in that perturbations occurring at the local level (to a particular aspect of the system) are generally mitigated such that they do not tend to lead to the collapse of the global coherence of the system. (For example, if a departmental lawyer chooses to quit the company and move elsewhere, there are procedures in place – for example, a substantial period of notice and procedures for internal and external recruitment - to ensure that the role of departmental lawyer ’survives’ the loss of the individual.)
Self-repairing: In the sense of global coherence to the organisation perpetuates itself beyond the extent to which any particular aspects of its structure remain original. The maintenance of global coherence in the face of absolute local change is best illustrated through the example of the Ship of Theseus: a ship that has existed so long, and been subject to so many repairs, that no part of it is original structure remains; the same logic underlies a particular pattern of joke: “This is my grandfather’s axe: the handle has been changed three times and the head four times but it’s still the same axe”. Unlike in these examples, however, the prefix self- suggests that the repair occurs due to internal rather than external volitions and actions.

 However, the author used the cautionary qualifier ‘may’ when introducing the analogy, because there is a danger of over-extending it, and drawing false inference about the qualities of bureaucracies (for example, with respect to the extent to which they  ‘reproduce’ or  ‘evolve’, the degree to which they exhibit internal coherence, and the extent to which they are ‘sentient’ or ‘intelligent’) by over-association with biology. If the reader believes the dangers of the analogy outweigh the benefits, then the above discussion may be disregarded.
[Qualities]
 [Formalisation/rationalisation, and obedience]
[Formalisation: making formally implicit information explicit]
[Rationalisation: reformulating explicit knowledge such that it converts decisions into automated procedures]
[Obedience: the agentic mode – depends entirely on social relationships]
Weber quotes
A lifeless machine is* congealed spirit. It is only* this fact that gives the machine the power to force men to serve it and thus to rule and determine their daily working lives, as in fact happens in factories. This same congealed spirit is, however, also embodied in that living machine which is represented by bureaucratic organisation with its specialisation of trained, technical work, its delimitation of areas of responsibility, its regulations and its graduated hierarchy of obedience. Combined with the dead machine, it is in the process of manufacturing the housing of that future serfdom to which, perhaps, men may have to submit powerlessly, just like the slaves in the ancient state of Egypt, if they consider that the ultimate and only value by which the conduct of their affairs is to be decided is good administration and provision for their needs by officials (that is ‘good’ in the purely technical sense of rational administration).  Bureaucracy achieves this, after all, incomparably better than another other structure of rule. This housing, so praised by our native littérateurs, will be augmented by shackles chaining each individual to his firm (the beginnings of this are to be found in so-called ‘welfare arrangements’), to his class (by an increasingly rigid structure of ownership) and perhaps at some time in the future to his occupation (by state provision for needs on a ‘liturgical’ principle, whereby associations structured along occupational lines carry a burden of state responsibilities). This housing would become even more indestructible if, in the social  area, as in the those states in the past where enforced labour existed, an organisation of the ruled based on their social and occupational states were to be attached (which in truth means subordinated) to the bureaucracy. An ‘organic’, that is an Oriental-Egyptian social structure would begin to emerge, but, in contrast to that ancient form, one which would be strictly rational as a machine.[3] [pp. 158-9]
An official who receives an order which, in his view, is wrong can – and should – raise objections. If his superior then insists on the instruction it is not merely the duty of the official, it is also a point of honour for his to carry out that instruction as if it corresponded to his own innermost conviction, thereby demonstrating that his sense of duty to his office overrides his individual wilfulness. It is irrelevant whether his superiors is a ‘public authority’ or a ‘corporation’, or an ‘assembly’ from which he has an imperative mandate. This is what is demanded by the spirit of office. [3][pp. 160-1]
[Conversion/Compliance]
Reading, writing, and rationalisation
[Rationalisation: both discretisation of complex functions, and formalisation – moving from tacit to explicit – of such functions]
[Instrumentalisation as enabler of greater compliance]

[Bourdieu and power of naming

    Language & Symbolic Power]

[Tilly: Why?

    Conventions + Authority = Codes]

Cause-Effect Accounts

Technical Accounts



[redrawn from [4] p. 19]
Formulas identify an appropriate correspondence between Y (the event, action or outcome at hand) and X (its antecedent), but enter little or not at all into the causal chain connecting Y to X. Cause-effect accounts trace causal lines from X to Y – even if we observers find those causal lines absurd or incomprehensible. “Popular” reasons obviously vary from one public to another, for example as a function of religiosity and religious creed. Specialized reasons likewise vary strikingly from discipline to discipline; theologians elaborate both codes and technical accounts that differ deeply from those proposed by medical practitioners.[4] [p. 20, my emphasis]
All four kinds of reasons commonly do relational work.[4][p. 19]

Conventions: conventionally accepted reasons for dereliction, deviation, distinction, or good fortune: my train was late, your turn finally came, she has breeding, he’s a lucky guy, and so on
Stories: explanatory narratives incorporating cause-effect accounts of unfamiliar phenomena or of exceptional events such as the 9/11 catastrophe, but also such as betrayal by a friend, winning a big prize, or meeting a high school classmate at Egypt’s Pyramids twenty years after graduation
Codes governing actions such as legal judgments, religious penance, or awarding of medals
Technical accounts of the outcomes in the first three: how a structural engineer, a dermatologist, or an orthopedic surgeon might explain what happened to [a victim] on the World Trade Center’s 88th floor after a hijacked aircraft struck the building on 9/11  [4][p. 15]
Within their own jurisdictions, professional givers promote and enforce the priority of codes and technical accounts over conventions and stories.
In particular, professional givers generally become skilled at translating conventions and stores into their preferred idioms, and at coaching other people to collaborate in that translation.
Hence the greater the professionalization of knowledge in any social setting, the greater the predominance of codes and technical accounts.
To the extent that relations between giver and received are distant and/or giver occupies a superior rank, giver provides formulas rather than cause-effect accounts.
Givers who offer formulas thereby claim superiority and/or distance.
Receivers ordinarily challenge such claims, when they do, by demanding cause-effect accounts.
Those demands typically take the forms of expressing scepticism about the proposed formula and asking for detail on how and why Y actually occurred. 
In the case of authoritatively delivered codes, however, a skilled received can also challenge the reasons given by deploying the code and demonstrating that giver has misused it.
Even in the presence of distance and/or inequality, to the extent that received has visible power to affect giver’s subsequent welfare, giver moves from formulas toward cause-effect accounts. [4][p. 25]

When it comes to codes, reasons given for actions cite their conformity to specialized sets of categories, procedures for ordering evidence, and rules of interpretation. Together, categories, procedures, and rules make up codes. [4][p.102]
