I don’t know enough Kant to tell whether this is just an attempt by Bourdieu to place his own ideas within an acceptable ‘geneology’ of continental philosophy. Orwell describes himself buying a glass of red wine at a cafe in France and sitting at a table outside, drinking the wine slowly, despite being on the edge of starvation and knowing that drinking the wine meant not being able to afford to eat. A Bourdieuian (?) perspective might suggest that, as a member of the ‘lower upper middle-classes’ (as he described himself!) Orwell placed a lot of value in signalling his continued membership to his class. Think of the title: an aspidistra was, at the time of writing, a social artefact which signalled membership of the middle-classes, displayed on the windowsill for passers-by. The reason the aspidistra was popular was apparently because it was one of the few plants which could survive the noxious fumes generated by gas and oil lamps. Having enough money to buy fuel to have a warm house was, of course, another indicator of membership of the middle-classes! Copyright has expired so should be able to get access to a free e-text to this This section is unfinished It’s at this kind of macroscale that electrical and electronic circuit theory operates, the laws of which - such as Kirchoff’s Laws - are deterministic rather than stochastic, and are extremely good at predicting electrical phenomena This is also covered in Ormerod’sButterfly Economics Schelling’sMicromotives and Macrobehaviouris one of a number of other books which give this example. It seems to have become a ‘classic’. This, of course, is the type of pattern Kuhn described to ‘scientific revolutions’: a new approach to a problem has much more instrumental capacity - capacity to change the environment and control a natural phenomena (or subset of a natural phenomena) in a way which someone wants. This increased instrumentalist capacity is recognised and investors begin to pour more resources into the new solution (or ‘paradigm’) until the only the new solution is funded and the original solution ‘dies out’. Though persons aren’t ‘converted’ from one ideology to another, something - whichcouldbe called progress - happens within science and technology.
This is something which could be considered within the structures of Game Theory. This approach is, of course, crypto-Darwinian, so has a rather hard-edged feel to it. This, of course, links well with the principles of geodemographics. ‘Birds of a feather flock together’. Less prosaicly people have significant ingroup preferences.

title: Heuristics and Attitudes
text:
Heuristics and Attitudes
According to the cognitive psychologists Daniel Kahneman and Amos Tversky, people are not (contraclassical economic theory) rational utility-maximizing agents, who seek to weigh up the costs and benefits of a range of available options in order to determine the ‘optimal solution’. Instead, we tend to use a number of intuitive heuristics – simple and efficient rules of thumb – which allow us to make decisions which, most of the time, in most scenarios, are effective in helping us arrive at quick and good answers to daily problems. They also lead to systematic biases when applied to the ‘wrong’ scenarios. One of the heuristics proposed by Kahneman and Tversky is the ‘representativeness heuristic’: that the likelihood of, say, a man being a criminal is proportional to how similar the man – who may or may not be a criminal – is to another man, who is a criminal. When researching attitudes towards different categories of the non-working, the representativeness heuristic may be useful in explaining why public opinion towards pensioners tends to be different than, say, those on incapacity benefit.
Understanding heuristics and biases helps one to understand more about the psychological origins of stereotypes. The stereotypes members of the public hold about other members of the public affect their attitudes to such people, and this is reflected in the popularity of different public policies. Popular policies tend to play up to popular attitudes and stereotypes certain groups of society have towards other groups. Such attitudes are often quite negative.
[My point is that there exist strong stereotypes which are connected with both work and home, and that understanding such stereotypes can be useful for understanding policies connected with these two concepts. As stated previously (I think), it seems paradoxical that, in an age where the amount of material which an individual can produce has increased greatly, and most of the material shortage problems which have plagued humanity for most of human existence have been solved, people still work. (Actually, perhaps, it’s not too paradoxical – I’ve not quite defined this correctly – so that the elements I’m juxtaposing are not actually dyadic – I need to redefine this slightly) Why do people care so much about employment and unemployment rates? Why do people tend to categorise others into ‘deserving’ and ‘undeserving’, even though there exist enough resources such that such judgements are no longer needed?
This sort of attitude seems to feed itself into the concept of macroeconomic productivity and growth: a country is judged to be successful if the total amount of money that has been counted as passing within it is greater than in a previous occasion. In this case it is said to have ‘grown’. But what’s interesting is that this monetary growth doesn’t necessarily correspond to the total amount of ‘stuff’ produced by the nation increasing. Macroeconomics also concerns itself with the concept of ‘efficiency’.
This concept of efficiency shares a strange relationship with changes in a nation’s population demographics. Persons are aged between 0 and some finite maximum age (which is increasing, but at a slowing rate, I believe), with differing proportions of the total population existing within different age ‘bins’. Persons below a certain age, and above a certain age, and traditionally of a certain gender, are considered to exist outside of the workforce; and persons within the boundary are said to comprise the potential workforce. A measure of the ‘productivity’ and the ‘strength’ of an economy that is often used, and considered important, is the unemployment rate, which is the proportion of all of the people within the bounded demographic region who have identified themselves as ‘not working’. There is also an employment rate, which is the proportion of persons within the bounded region who have identified themselves, indirectly through a set of legal and bureaucratic mechanisms, as belonging to the ‘active workforce’. For one thing, the ‘unemployed’ and the ‘employed’ are not complements of one another, and the fact this is often intuitively assumed means that reporting changes in one can be used as a means of providing a misleading inference about the other. This is not the case. Another rough measure of economic ‘efficiency’ often used is the ratio between the number of persons in the potential workforce to the number of persons not in the potential workforce. The underlying principle seems to be that those in the ‘workforce’ are considered to be ‘supporting’ or ‘carrying’ those who are not, that those who are not are considered parasitic upon those who are. There the ratio of workers to ‘non-workers’ is high, then the economy is considered healthy, or to have good potential. Where the converse is true, labour economists suck air through their teeth and predict the collapse of the modern world.
Population demographics are interesting to consider visually: imagine, within the great statistical mass the population histograms represent, a single individual. This individual will move at a constant rate from age zero to some unknown but finite countable finite final age, at which he or she will sense to be. At the demographic level that is all we are: little dots or thinly sliced pieces of line that move from left to right at a constant rate. As we move along from left to right we stand on all other people – or the idealised statistical pseudo-people – the lines and dots – who represent us, in an unwavering vertical line. As everyone else who moves along the demographic chart does so at the same rate (i.e. we have this characteristic of aging one year per year, which Thomas Shelling considered a strange characteristic!) it is as if the whole line moves from left to right. As the vertical line moves inexorably from left to right, its height – the aggregated number of dots or thinly sliced pieces of line – changes. In a closed society – where there is no immigration – this change is always downwards: From an initial level, there is a slow but inexorable decline as one moves from left to right. Mathematically, this could be represented as follows: Population(birthyear, currentyear) = A.exp-(decay function)(currentyear-birthyear) A is the number of births in the particular birthyear, and the decay function could either be a constant, of a function comprising some other variables. ]

title: Early PhD Direction: Simulating Geographies of Homelessness and Worklessness
text:
Context: This document was written during the transition from the MA to the PhD (2005–2006). It represents the early theoretical direction for the PhD — an ambitious attempt to bridge complexity theory, behavioural economics, and critical social theory to understand the spatial dynamics of disadvantage. The PhD later pivoted from this direction towards an analysis of the Pathways to Work programme, resulting in the submitted thesis Work and its Other.
This extended research document is presented here as an optional appendix to the PhD materials. It provides insight into the intellectual territory that preceded and informed the final thesis, even though the thesis ultimately took a different direction.
The document is explicitly a work in progress, with visible authorial notes, structural uncertainty, and bracketed working commentary preserved as-is.

title: Preface
text:
(Preface)
In the middle of 2005 I applied to conduct funded PhD research with the provisional title, “Simulating Geographies of Homelessness and Worklessness”. When I applied, I was fairly sure I knew what the latter two nouns meant. Now, in 2006, I’m less sure: as I’ve explored the conceptual, theoretical, technical and empirical framework underlying the ontology of these negations I have, as one would have hoped, learned more. As I have learnt more, however, I’ve ‘known’ less: clarity has begotten confusion, and a clear path has become a snaking wilderness.
My initial task within this thesis will be to attempt to instil within you, the reader, a semblance of the confusion I have felt over the course of my research. Once I have taken you into the wilderness, I will then attempt to show a possible route out. My journey is rarely travelled because, within academia, despite many people’s best efforts and intentions, a certain kind of tribalism seems more the ‘rule’ than the ‘exception’: having sojourned within both the applied physical sciences and the postmodern humanities, I know first hand the differences in attitude which appear both a cause and effect of a hard division between ‘hard’ and ‘soft’ disciplines.
The social sciencesarehard: they’re hard because social phenomena is inherently dynamic and complex, because the scales of time and space involved are very large, because we can’t run ‘controlled experiments’ on society (and where such attempts have been made the consequences have tended towards disaster), because the data (or our tools for data analysis, or both) are inadequate, and because the level of certainty we usually want from our investigations are less than we can achieve.
They’re also hard because they’re important. No one can elect not to be a social being, and not be part of a social world. Uncertainty about the meaning and direction of our existence is difficult to accept because, ultimately, this is all we are, and all we have The polymath economist John Maynard Keynes is alleged to have asked the physicist Max Planck whether he had ever considered social research: his reply was that he hadn’t because the maths were too hard. (see Ormerod, 1998: 191 1. Ormerod, P.,Butterfly Economics: A New General Theory of Social and Economic Behaviour. 1998, London: Faber & Faber. 217.)

The working draft from PhD Year 2 (March 2008 DVD backup) contains the theoretical framework in its most expansive form. The thesis was structured in three sections and eight chapters:

title: PhD: Work and its Other
text:
Intellectual trajectory
The PhD evolved significantly from its original proposal. The 2005 application described two related but distinct projects:

The funded project (White Rose Studentship): Simulating Geographies of Homelessness and Worklessness — applied microsimulation modelling in Leeds, Sheffield, and York
The personal research interest: The Evolution and Utility of Government — ambitious theoretical work on evolutionary politics and decision-making

By 2008, the thesis had become a theoretical hybrid: the proposal’s ambitious framework (Damasio on consciousness, Weber on bureaucracy, Diamond on government evolution, Polanyi on economics) was being applied to analyse the Pathways to Work policy as a concrete case study. The pre-revision chapters show this theoretical scaffold being constructed.

title: PhD: Work and its Other
text:
Early direction (2006)

Before becoming a statistician and data scientist, I pursued an unusual academic path: from Electronic Engineering through Critical Theory to Sociology and Human Geography. These three works trace that intellectual journey.

These documents were recovered from personal CD-ROM and DVD backups dating from 2003 to 2011. The original files were Microsoft Word documents (.doc/.docx), converted to markdown using textutil (macOS) and Python processing. Some content was lost in conversion:

Figures and diagrams: The BEng thesis referenced CorelDRAW graphics and MATLAB figures; these are shown as placeholders in the web version but the original image files (430+ BMP/EMF) are preserved in the archive.
Equations: Mathematical notation embedded via Microsoft Equation Editor was stripped during conversion; equation references are preserved as placeholders.
The MA thesis is a working draft with visible authorial notes — this is presented as-is, preserving the texture of work in progress.
The PhD is presented in two versions: the 2008 working draft (pre-revision) and the final submitted PDF (2009).

The underlying archive, including all original MATLAB code, test images, and document files, is maintained in a private vault. The MATLAB codebase for the BEng project is being ported to Python for interactive demonstration.

Abstract
The purpose of this project is to create a working prototype for a machine vision system capable of being used in certain types of machine calibration and related problems. Specifically, the prototype needs to be able to identify and determine the real-life displacement of an object within fixed boundaries on a two-dimensional plane, and provide information to the prototype user.

title: BEng: An Investigation Into Machine Vision Calibration Systems
text:
Key results
The system achieved:

0.01% accuracy relative to the bounded area for object displacement measurement
Successful single-camera operation (improved over original two-camera design)
0.5mm precision in real-world displacement measurement (actual: 6.0mm right, 4.0mm down; measured: 5.5mm, 4.0mm)
Robust performance across varying lighting conditions and with non-critical objects present
crumbs: BEng: An Investigation Into Machine Vision Calibration Systems

title: BEng: An Investigation Into Machine Vision Calibration Systems
text:
Technical approach
The project combined two complementary techniques:

Digital Image Correlation (DIC) — using 2D fast Fourier transforms to detect displacement between image pairs
Fiduciary Marker Analysis — using colour-based detection with edge finding, dilation, hole-filling, and erosion to identify reference points

The system operated in two stages:

Stage 1 (Prototype Positioning): Detecting and correcting camera-to-plane misalignment using fiduciary markers
Stage 2 (Object Displacement): Using DIC to find the actual object displacement, with rigid-body compensation

title: Methods and Process
text:
4
 Methods and Process
3.1 Section Introduction
This section considers the processes required for the prototype system to deliver the required results. This involves both a schematic description- involving inputs, processes and outputs- and a more detailed description of each of these processes.
Overall Process
3.2.1 Introduction
The prototype must allow the user to identify what the object displacement is from its current location, and that on a stored (archived) image. For this, it needs to know the displacement of the object, in the plane, and in a real-life quantity such as millimetres.
As stated previously (Section 1.3.1), the problem splits broadly into three tasks:
Locating the plane: Positioning the prototype device correctly relative to the object plane.
Locating the object: Finding the position of the object within the fixed boundaries of the area on the plane.
Finding object displacement: Finding how far the object has moved in the plane from its original position.
Tasks 2 and 3 can be handled at the same time: One first has to locate the object in order to determine its disparity, and thus any object image created in task 2 can subsequently be used to determine object displacement. Thus tasks 2 and 3 are combined together, in the code, into what is called Stage 2.
Whereas tasks 2 and 3 are best combined, the task of positioning the prototype device correctly relative to the plane  (Task 1) is best handled if broken down into a number of sub-stages.
Stage 1: Prototype Positioning
Ideally, the camera should be positioned such that it faces the bounded area of the plane directly; and at a distance from the plane far enough that all of the marker features are in view, but near enough that the pixel resolution of the camera device does not greatly impair the ability for the device to determine small degrees of object disparity (as the distance increases, so does the area of real space represented by a single pixel).
When dealing with a simple rectangular pattern of features, as in the test case, the camera should ideally be positioned such that the features are as follows: [Figure — original CorelDRAW graphic not available in web version]
Ideal Image Position
[Fig 3.2.1]
One could perform a very basic position detection system by attempting to determine that the centre of the features is at the centre of the image, and that the position of the features in the image are equidistant from this central point; but this would only tell the user whether the camera is positioned correctly, or not correctly. To aid the user in positioning the device, the system needs to be able to tell the user both whether the camera is positioned correctly or not, and – if not correctly aligned – how the image is mis-positioned, and what action the user should take to rectify this.
There are a number of specific ways that the camera could be mis-positioned. It could be: parallel to the plane but rotated slightly in this plane (1); rotated slightly in the ‘vertical’ plane perpendicular to that of the object plane (2); or rotated slightly in the ‘horizontal’ plane perpendicular to that of the object plane (3): [Figure — original CorelDRAW graphic not available in web version]
[Figure — original CorelDRAW graphic not available in web version]
[Figure — original CorelDRAW graphic not available in web version]
(1) (2) (3)
[Fig. 3.2.2]
The actual skew of the image is likely to be a combination of these three types of rotation: [Figure — original CorelDRAW graphic not available in web version]
Combination of skews
[Fig. 3.2.3]
Aligning the camera can be handed as a three-stage process. For demonstration purposes, the prototype only works to determine type (1) rotation, and then type (3) rotation. Type (2) rotation is analogous to type (3) rotation, and so showing that the method works for type (3) rotation means that type (2) problems can also be solved.
Thus, the process used to determine whether the camera is correctly positioned assumes the camera is first positioned correctly vertically: that it is at the correct height relative to the plane, and not at an angle. 
Assuming this, the code first corrects type (1) rotation (the Main 1a function), then type (3) rotation (The Main 1b function). A full system would include a third function to determine type (2) rotation, but the process for this is analogous to correcting type (3) rotation, and so the system does not have to demonstrate this in order to demonstrate its effectiveness.
Stage 2: Determining Object Displacement
Once correctly positioned, the next stage is to use digital image correlation (DIC) methods to determine the displacement between the current object position, and that in an archived image. 
Performing a straight DIC comparison of the two images (the current image, and the archive one where the object is correctly positioned) is not an effective way of determining object displacement. A DIC output of a full image may suggest a ‘match’ at a number of positions, for a number of reasons. If there is a repeated pattern on the image plane then the DIC method will match each repeated feature in the pattern in one image, to both the equivalent and the adjacent repeated features in the other image. 
Although Stage 1 of the process ensures the camera is at the right angle relative to the object area, it does not ensure the camera is in a completely central position regarding shifts in the direction of the plane. The camera position of the current image may be displaced horizontally and vertically. To ensure that this form of displacement, known as rigid body displacement, does not get confused by the computer with object displacement, DIC is performed on the marker masks of both the archive and the current images. This produces an unambiguous figure for this rigid body displacement. Using this, versions of the two images – current and archive – are created that compare only the region of the images that are common to both, and in the object intensity plane.
A DIC is then performed of these two adjusted object images, and the displacement figure given by finding the position of the greatest intensity in the DIC output used as the object displacement in pixels.
To convert the displacement in pixels to a displacement in millimetres (or another real unit), a conversion scale is created. This involves finding the distance in pixels between markers, and comparing them with the values given for distance between the same markers as part of the image geometry information given at the start. In the test example- four markers marking out the corners of a rectangle- the distances between the centres of the two ‘rows’ and ‘columns’ of markers are compared with the height and width of the rectangle given in the marker geometry information.
Flow Diagrams
The following pages provide flow diagrams of the Matlab program written for this project. Further details on the implementation of these processes are provided via the example case in the next section (4.2.1), which shows how a single test case is analysed and interpreted by each consecutive stage. 
One may find the test example more useful than the following process schematics when first considering how the prototype system works, and the schematics useful in determining how the system operates in the general case. 
In attempting to understand the actual techniques used to create markers and determine their centre positions, one should refer to sections 2.3 and 4.2.2.1 for explanations of the theory behind and examples resulting from this.
Black curved rectangles indicate overall start and end processes. Red curved rectangles indicate intermediate (function level) start and end processes. Green diamonds indicate decision nodes. Normal rectangles indicate self-contained processes. Grey rectangles with triangles within indicate internal data acquisition stages (Bracketed entries indicate optional data). Irregular parallelograms indicate external inputs (such as camera image acquisition) if bold, and external outputs (feedback to user) if italic.

This stage combines both rigid body disparity determination and object disparity determination (stage 2), and conversion of object disparity figures into real-life values (stage 2) into a single function. As the test shape is of a rectangle, it then converts this single mask into masks of the four quarters, and finds the centres of each the marker objects in each. From this, it determines the position of the centres of the markers in the overall mask, and thus the image. See section 4.2.2.1 for an example of this function*

title: Introduction
text:
1
Introduction
1.1 Overall Introduction
The overall aim of this project is to create a functioning prototype for device capable of determining the displacement of an ‘object,’ within a two-dimensional plane, between its current position, and its position at a previous time: [Figure — original CorelDRAW graphic not available in web version]
The Problem: A prototype device needs to be able to know how much an object has moved in a two-dimensional plane from a previous occasion
[Fig. 1.1]
The prototype should be able to report to the user how much the object is displaced from its original position, and thus allow the user to adjust the re-position the object into its original location
1.2 Examples
Introduction
Before continuing, it is useful to consider some real-life scenarios where finding a solution to the problem described is useful.
1.2.2 A Specific Example
Consider a machine designed to solder a component on a circuit board. The machine has a circuit holder, and under which a tool head – holding the soldering device –, moved by two pairs of motors normal to each other, so that the tool head moves in the same plane as the circuit. After finishing soldering a component, the tool head returns to its rest position. If the actual rest position of the tool head is significantly different to the calibrated one, then the soldering device will solder in the wrong position and the component will not be soldered correctly:
[Figure — original CorelDRAW graphic not available in web version]
A Specific Example: The tool head moves within a two-dimensional plane. When it has finished machining a part, it returns to a rest position. If the rest position it actually returns to is different to the intended rest position, then all machining operations by the device will be in the wrong place.
[Fig. 1.2]
To calibrate using the calibrating device:
The four extremities of the rectangular circuit holder could be marked with a colour used nowhere else on or near the machine (such as with a fluorescent colour); the soldering device could be removed, leaving the tool head, which could be marked at a specific position with another distinct, but different, colour.
The camera could be positioned, by hand, roughly above machine, such that four rectangular fixed points on the soldering device (and thus the points marking the machine geometry) are visible to the calibrating device. An image could then be taken, and the position of these machine geometry points determined. If the shape, formed by the points, matches the geometry of points defining the machine input previously into the calibrating device, then the calibrating device is known to be at the correct angle to the machine. 
Using existing digital image correlation (DIC) techniques, any disparity between a pair of images, where the disparity is local to part of the image-pair, can be determined, and distinguished between disparity common throughout the image-pair (Which is known as ‘rigid-body’ disparity, and may occur, for example, if one of the images is taken when one image is taken further to the right than the other image). This local disparity, common only to a small portion of the image-image pair, indicates that something within the machine, and not the machine itself nor the camera, has moved – Thus, hopefully, the tool head disparity has been identified. 
From this information, the actual position of the tool had can be determined and, as before, compared with the desired position, and the disparity output to the user as a real value.
1.2.3 Some Other Examples
Other examples of real-life problems that require this kind of object displacement within a two-dimensional plane to be identified are as follows:
Quality Control: When a circuit board has been made, an image of the board could be taken, and the position of the components on the board compared with those taken on a correctly positioned test image. If any of the components are significantly out of position then the board could be rejected and the machinery that places the misaligned components investigated.
Robot Tool-head Positioning: Using a pair of the devices, placed at different positions, the position of a tool-head of a robot, or other machine whose field of movement in three-dimensions, could be compared with that when properly in positions. Finding the displacement of an object in three dimensions can be solved as a set of Cartesian problems involving displacement in two-dimensional planes.
Abstraction Of Problem
Introduction
The problem to be solved could be considered analytically as involving three stages:
Locating the object plane: The prototype device needs to be able to determine its own position relative to the plane the object exists on.
Locating the object: The prototype device needs to be able to locate the position of the object within the plane.
Finding object displacement: The prototype device needs to be able to determine the displacement in the plane of the object, between its current position, and the correct position.
Each stage of the problem will now be described individually:
Stage One: Locating The Plane
Consider a three-dimensional space: Within the space is a two dimensional plane and the calibrating device. The calibrating device could be thought considered as a point Pcam, and a unit vector Vcam, located at point Pcam. The point Pcam marks the camera position, and the unit vector Vcam marks the direction the camera faces.
On the plane is a bounded area A, at the centre of which is a point Pplane, from which a unit vector Vnormal, of the normal to the plane, could be located.  
Starting at Pcam, one could consider a viewing cone, in direction Vcam, to emanate. 
The camera should be positioned so that the bounded area A lies within the viewing cone. 
Certain features of the bounded area A should be identified. With these, the direction of the normal vector of the plane Vnormal, and the position of point Pplane, should be identified.
With this information, the camera should be positioned such that Vnormal = -Vcam, and that both Pplane and Pcam lie at some position within this vector: i.e., that the camera directly faces the area of interest.
The process for positioning the camera with respect to the plane could itself be completed in a number of stages: First to locate the whole of the whole of the area A, then to ensure the camera faces the plane normally in the x-y axis (for example), then the y-z axis. 
Stage Two: Locating The Object
Assuming the camera is now facing the area of interest, the next stage is to locate the position of the object within the bounded area A. This could be considered as follows:
An object, O, exists somewhere within the fixed boundaries of the area on the two-dimensional plane. 
A certain feature marking part of the area boundary is located and considered the origin point (0,0) of the area. Other features of the area boundary are identified, and used later. [Figure — original CorelDRAW graphic not available in web version]
The area A: Features marking parts of the boundary of this area are identified, and their positions within the camera images are stored
[Fig. 1.3.1]
The object of interest, existing within the area, is identified, and its position in the camera image is found relative to the origin feature: [Figure — original CorelDRAW graphic not available in web version]
The current position of the position within the bounded area is located in the camera image
[Fig. 1.3.2]** equation [Eqn. 1.3.1]
1.3.4 Stage Three: Finding Object Disparity
Having located the current object position, it now has to be compared with its original position, and the displacement between these two positioned provided to the user. There are, broadly, two ways to do this:
The position of the movable object in the image at the present moment could be translated into a real position (in millimetres, centimetres etc) by comparing its position to those of fixed marker objects (the identified boundary features) whose real geometries (the real-life distances and angles between objects) are known. This could be compared with the real-life position of the movable object within the area when correctly positioned, and the displacement between these two positions output to the user. equation [Eqn. 1.3.2]
An image of the object in the area, when in the correct position, could be stored in the prototype system. This could be compared directly with the image of the object in the area at the present moment, and the disparity between the two objects in the two images (in pixels) determined. This disparity in pixels could then be translated into a real-life disparity by comparing the distances of the fixed boundary features in the images, with their actual distances between these fixed features. Again, this essentially provides a scale, which can be used to convert between pixel disparities and real-life disparities. [Figure — original CorelDRAW graphic not available in web version]
An image of the object when in the correct position
[Fig. 1.3.3] equation [Eqn. 1.3.3]
The second approach is used in the project, because using this approach means:
The user does not actually have to know the real-life position of the object when in the correct position, just have taken an image of it at this position to cross-reference with.
The marker does not have to be painted or connected to something of a specific colour or shape for the prototype to identify, which would be difficult to arrange if the object is an intricate part of a machine. 
The Test Problem
For the purpose of testing the prototype in this project, the bounded area used was of a simple rectangle, the boundary features being the four corners. The distances between the centres of the boundary features (i.e. the height and width of the rectangle) were set and known by the prototype system for referencing with the position of these features in images: [Figure — original CorelDRAW graphic not available in web version]
Diagram of the area and boundary features used
[Fig. 1.4.1]
Picture of the actual test area
[Fig. 1.4.2]
1.5 Possible Techniques To Solving The Problem
*** 1.5.1 Introduction***
Two basic techniques were considered to solve the test problem: Digital image correlation (DIC) based on fast-Fourier transforms (FFTs), and fiduciary marker analysis:
*** 1.5.2 Digital Image Correlation***
Digital image correlation is based on two-dimensional fast Fourier transforms (FFTs). It allows two similar images to be compared to locate areas of disparity between the two images as a whole, or sub-areas of the two images.
Presently, DIC techniques have been used and developed by the university to provide highly accurate and comparatively inexpensive methods to determine minute relative displacements in objects due to known and controlled variables (such as the physical warping of microchips due to heat generated during operation) under controlled (laboratory) conditions. 
Applying DIC techniques towards this end is a potentially inexpensive alternative to developing ever more intricate cameras, as it relies more heavily on software than hardware: For example, sub-pixel algorithm techniques have been developed by the university to allow much more displacement information to be acquired from the same physical device, and thus allow more accurate data to be acquired at the same material expense. 
In laboratory conditions, the position of the imaging device relative to the two-dimensional plane on which the image-pair disparity occurs can be controlled and known to a high degree. In the workplace, this is not the case, and so the imaging device needs be able to have some means of sensing its own position relative to the plane of interest. DIC techniques exist that allow this plane positioning information to be determined, but the most obvious application for DIC techniques is determining the position of the object within the plane, when the imaging device is facing the plane directly. 
A more detailed explanation of the theory behind DIC is given in the next section (Section 2.2).
1.5.3 Fiduciary Marker Analysis
An imaging device is capable of distinguishing between objects within the image if they have distinctive properties. They can have a size, shape, or colour that distinguishes them from other parts of the image. If the objects within the image can be reliably distinguished from others they can be used as fiduciary markers, to be used as reference points to determine displacement, scale and position of both objects and the imaging device:
If the relative positions of a number of distinguishable markers are fixed and the position of a camera is variable, they can be used to determine the position of the camera relative to the markers. 
If the positions of the markers vary, but the camera position is fixed, they can be used to determine the real displacements of objects and parts on which the markers exist.
Brief Discussion Of Approaches
Both DIC and fiduciary marker analysis can be applied to solving all three areas of the problem. Each has distinct advantages and disadvantages when applied to different types of task, due to the way they work:
  ****
  ****


  **
  


  **
  


  **
  


  **
  


  **
  







Problem feature:
Best method to use:




Object plane is an amorphous, fixed surface
DIC


Object plane is a smooth surface, or has as repeating pattern throughout
Fiduciary markers


Object is of a fixed and easily machine- distinguishable shape or colour
Fiduciary markers


Object within boundaries is not distinguishable through shape or colour, but is the only feature within the shape that moves
DIC


Distinctive fixed markers cannot be used in nearby vicinity of object
DIC



For the project, a combination of both techniques was used.
